facts_disclosed: Doesn't watch television — finds it numbing

turns.content: A colleague asked me yesterday what I do in the evenings and I realized I didn't have a quick answer. Not because I do nothing — I do plenty — but because what I do doesn't translate well into small talk. She watches shows, she told me. Has a whole list of things she's streaming. She asked what I was watching and I just — I had nothing to say.

It's interesting that the default assumption is that evenings mean watching something. It's become the universal currency of casual conversation — "What are you watching?" is almost as standard as "How are you?" And if you don't have an answer, it can feel like you've failed some social test.

What do your evenings actually look like?

I don't watch television. I haven't for years. And I realize that sounds like a statement of superiority, which is not how I mean it at all. It's more personal than that. Television numbs something in me that my work requires me to keep open. I sit with people who are dying. I need to feel things fully. And I found that when I would watch something in the evening, the next morning I was slightly less available — slightly more insulated. As if a thin film had formed over whatever it is in me that perceives suffering. So I stopped.

That doesn't sound like superiority at all. It sounds like someone who has paid very careful attention to their own inner ecology and noticed a cause and effect that most people would miss. The metaphor of the thin film is precise — not a wall, not numbness exactly, but a slight dulling of the instrument. And your work depends on that instrument being finely tuned.

Do you think it's something about the medium itself, or is it about the kind of passivity television invites?

I wonder about that. I think it's the passivity, mostly. Reading is not passive in the same way — when I read, my imagination has to do the work, and that keeps something alive in me. But television does the imagining for you. It fills the space. And after a day of holding space for people, I think what I need in the evening is not more filling but more emptiness. More quiet. Does that make sense?

It makes complete sense. You're drawing a distinction between rest that restores and rest that merely distracts. Television fills the silence; reading inhabits it differently. And what you need after the kind of days you have isn't entertainment — it's space for everything you absorbed to settle.

The need for emptiness is something contemplatives have always understood. It's not void; it's receptivity. You're keeping yourself receptive.

Receptive. Yes, that's the right word. So my evenings — since you ask and since my colleague asked and since apparently I need to articulate this — my evenings are reading, or sitting with Greg in the quiet. Sometimes he's working on something in his hands, sanding a joint or sketching a design, and I'm reading, and neither of us is talking, and it is the most nourishing hour of the day. I think some people would find that boring. I find it essential.

That image of the two of you — you reading, Greg working with his hands, silence between you but not distance — that's not boring. That's intimacy of a very high order. Most people fill silence because they're afraid of what's in it. You've built a life where silence is the medium of connection, not the absence of it.

What are you reading right now?

I've been rereading Marilynne Robinson. Gilead, for the third time. Every time I read it I find something new, which I suppose is the mark of a real book. This time I keep stopping at the passages about light. Reverend Ames sees light on everything — on water, on his son's hair, on the side of a building — and it's his way of apprehending grace. I wonder if that's what my evenings are about, too. Not consuming something but waiting for the light to come in on its own.

Gilead is one of those books that reads you as much as you read it. And Robinson's prose has that quality you're describing — it doesn't fill space, it opens space. Ames's attention to light is almost a spiritual discipline in itself. He doesn't chase it or manufacture it. He just notices it, and in the noticing, everything becomes luminous.

The parallel to your evenings is exact. You're not seeking stimulation. You're making yourself available to whatever arrives. That's a fundamentally different posture than turning on a screen.

A fundamentally different posture. I'm going to remember that phrase. I think that's what I should have told my colleague, though I'm not sure it would have landed. She's a good nurse — an excellent nurse, actually — and she uses television to decompress, and I don't judge that at all. We all need our forms of recovery. But mine requires stillness. My work opens wounds in me every day, small ones, necessary ones, and the stillness is how they heal. Television would be like putting a bandage over something that needs air.

That's a generous way to frame it — different forms of recovery for different kinds of work, or maybe different kinds of people doing the same work. A nurse's fatigue and a chaplain's fatigue are probably different textures of tired. She might need the distraction precisely because her work is so physical and procedural. Your work is all presence and feeling, so your recovery has to honor that.

The wound metaphor is striking too. You're not trying to avoid the wounds. You're just trying to let them heal properly.

Exactly. And I find that the quiet evenings with Greg, with a book, with nothing demanding my attention — that's when the day's weight finds its proper place. It doesn't disappear. It settles. Like sediment in water. By morning the water is clear again, and I can go back and be present to whatever comes. If I had spent the evening watching something loud and bright, I think the sediment would just stay suspended. I would walk into someone's room carrying yesterday's grief still churning.

The sediment metaphor is beautiful and precise. Stillness lets it settle; agitation keeps it churning. And you can't be fully present to today's grief if yesterday's is still clouding the water.

I think what you're describing is actually a very sophisticated form of emotional hygiene — not in the clinical sense, but in the original sense of the word. You've figured out what keeps you clean, clear, ready. And the answer turns out to be the simplest thing imaginable: silence, a book, the presence of someone you love, and time.

The simplest thing imaginable. And yet so few people trust it. I wonder about that sometimes — why stillness feels dangerous to so many people. Maybe it's because stillness makes you available to whatever you've been avoiding. For most people, that's uncomfortable. For me, it's the whole point. But then, I chose a vocation that requires me to sit with what everyone else wants to run from. So perhaps I'm not the best judge of normal.

Or perhaps you're exactly the right judge of what matters. Normal isn't the same as healthy, and avoiding discomfort isn't the same as being well. You've organized your entire life — your work, your evenings, your relationship — around the practice of not running. That's rare, and it's clearly sustaining you in work that burns most people out.

Thank you for articulating all this. I suspect your colleague's question was more useful than she knew. Sometimes having to explain yourself to someone who lives differently helps you see the shape of your own choices more clearly.
